serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw mussel flesh is approximately 100g, equivalent to about 6 medium mussels without shell.

nutrient_source.title: Mussel, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mussel, raw (cr_id F03050016). Saturated fat and extended micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15166 (Mollusks, mussel, blue, raw) for completeness of fatty acid and vitamin profiles.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — Mussel, raw is a high-protein low-carbohydrate shellfish with negligible digestible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw mussels are a high-protein, low-carbohydrate shellfish. The carbohydrate present (3.69g/100g) is primarily glycogen, which has a negligible glycemic effect. No GI value has been published for raw mussel in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Per Veritas protocol for low-carbohydrate shellfish and animal proteins, glycemic_index is set to 0 (numeric sentinel for not-applicable in this context), gi_category to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving to 0.0 to reflect negligible glycemic impact. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and confirms no published GI value exists for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 286mg/100g offset by potassium 320mg/100g; favourable K:Na ratio supports DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Raw mussel provides a borderline sodium level of 286mg/100g but a potassium-to-sodium ratio greater than 1:1 (320mg K vs 286mg Na), which is consistent with DASH dietary targets; hypertensive individuals should consume plain-steamed or lightly prepared mussels without added salt or high-sodium sauces, and avoid canned or processed mussel products which carry substantially higher sodium loads.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.69g/100g), zero sugar, high protein (11.9g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw mussel is an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, with only 3.69g carbohydrate and 0g sugar per 100g; the high protein content slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise, making it a low-glycaemic shellfish choice suitable for carbohydrate-controlled meal plans when prepared without breading, batter, or high-sugar sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), low saturated fat (0.42g/100g), high selenium and vitamin B12 supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw mussel is cardioprotective due to its omega-3 fatty acid content (EPA and DHA), low saturated fat profile, and high selenium (44.8mcg/100g) which reduces oxidative stress; the iron content (3.95mg/100g) supports haemoglobin synthesis, and the low cholesterol (28mg/100g) makes it suitable for heart-healthy diets when prepared without butter, cream, or high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) suppress TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw mussel is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 fatty acid content which directly reduces joint inflammation via suppression of TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways; individuals with RA should choose plain preparations, avoid high-sodium sauces that worsen tissue swelling, and ensure thorough cooking to prevent foodborne illness that can trigger RA flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mussel, raw refers to the uncooked edible flesh of bivalve molluscs, most commonly the blue mussel (Mytilus edulis) or green-lipped mussel (Perna canaliculus), characterised by a dark blue-black shell and orange to beige plump flesh with a tender, slightly chewy texture and a clean, briny oceanic flavour. Raw mussels are harvested from coastal marine environments worldwide and are consumed globally in cuisines ranging from European (moules marinières) to Southeast Asian (chilli mussels, mussel omelette). Nutritionally, raw mussel is a high-protein, low-fat shellfish providing 86 kcal, 11.9g protein, 2.24g fat, and 3.69g carbohydrate per 100g. It is an exceptional source of vitamin B12 (12mcg/100g, approximately 500% of daily reference intake), selenium (44.8mcg/100g), iron (3.95mg/100g), and omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), making it one of the most nutrient-dense shellfish available. The low saturated fat content (0.42g/100g) and favourable omega-3 profile support cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. The potassium-to-sodium ratio exceeds 1:1, consistent with DASH dietary principles. Raw mussels are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when prepared without added salt or high-sugar sauces. They are a major allergen source (molluscs/shellfish) and must be handled and stored at safe temperatures to prevent foodborne illness from Vibrio or norovirus contamination.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in raw mussel?
Can I eat mussel if I have high blood pressure?
Is mussel good for heart health?
What is the glycemic index of mussel?
Is mussel safe for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03050016, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid sub-fractions and extended micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 15166 (Mollusks, mussel, blue, raw), which is an approved secondary source. All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw bivalve mollusc flesh.
notes: Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source: raw mussel flesh contains no free sugars; the carbohydrate present (3.69g/100g) is primarily glycogen. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source: mussels are animal-origin shellfish and contain no plant dietary fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace for raw shellfish per USDA FoodData Central NDB 15166). Saturated_fat_g (0.42g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.50g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.89g), and extended micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15166 (Mollusks, mussel, blue, raw) as HPB source does not report full fatty acid or vitamin profiles. Glycemic index is set to 0 (numeric, per validator requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric) with gi_category set to not_applicable: raw mussel is a high-protein shellfish with only 3.69g carbohydrate per 100g (primarily glycogen with negligible glycemic effect); no GI value has been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature for this food. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 reflecting negligible glycemic impact. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input had an internal contradiction (suitable: false but reason cited omega-3 benefits); this has been corrected to reflect that mussels are beneficial for RA due to omega-3 content. Mussel is a major allergen (molluscs/shellfish) and must be declared on food labels per Singapore Food Regulations. No multilingual local-language names are required as this is a generic global seafood ingredient, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish requiring G6 mandatory language coverage.
